text.title: Feature Discovery Process
text.summary: Six-step feature discovery process: collect from four sources, Kano categorization, Opportunity Scoring, effort estimation, RICE prioritization, and pre-build validation.

section.title: Step 1: Collect from Four Source Types

rule.statement: Collect feature ideas from all four source types before scoring: Customer Research (interviews, support tickets, churn conversations, NPS detractors), Usage Analytics (adoption rates, drop-off points, error rates), Competitive Analysis (competitor features and gaps, negative reviews), Market Trends (industry reports, technology and regulatory changes).
rule.rationale: Single-source discovery creates blind spots; analytics reveal what users do but not why; interviews reveal why but not how often; competitive analysis reveals expectations but not differentiation opportunities.

rule.statement: Cap the candidate list at 20 items per discovery cycle — scoring 50+ features creates decision paralysis.
rule.rationale: A list longer than 20 means the collection phase wasn't selective enough; the scoring step becomes a distraction rather than a decision tool.

rule.statement: Categorize each feature into one of four Kano types: Must-have (basic expectations; absence causes dissatisfaction), Performance (more = more satisfaction), Delight/Excitement (unexpected; creates delight if present), Indifferent (customers don't care). Build Must-haves before Delighters.
rule.rationale: Delighters built on a shaky Must-have foundation fail to retain customers; users who can't log in are not impressed by AI suggestions.

rule.statement: Score each feature: Opportunity = Importance + max(Importance - Satisfaction, 0). Scale Importance and Satisfaction 1-10. High opportunity = high importance AND low satisfaction. Flag any score above 10 as high priority.
rule.rationale: Features users find highly important but poorly served by current solutions are the highest-value targets; the formula penalizes well-served needs and rewards underserved ones.

section.title: Step 4: Effort Estimation
section.p: Effort categories: XS (less than 1 day), S (1-3 days), M (1-2 weeks), L (2-4 weeks), XL (1-3 months). Engineering lead must validate all effort estimates before RICE scores drive roadmap decisions — agent estimates are not acceptable inputs.

rule.statement: Compute RICE = (Reach x Impact x Confidence) / Effort. Reach = users per quarter; Impact = 0.25/0.5/1/2/3; Confidence = 50%/80%/100%; Effort = person-weeks. Sort descending; validate top 3 before committing to build.
rule.rationale: RICE prevents high-effort low-reach features from crowding out low-effort high-reach features; the confidence multiplier forces honest assessment of evidence quality.

rule.statement: Validate top-RICE features before committing: Fake door test (low effort, medium confidence), Prototype (medium effort, high confidence), Wizard of Oz (medium effort, high confidence), Limited beta (high effort, very high confidence), Pre-sales (medium effort, very high confidence).
rule.rationale: RICE scoring is based on estimates; pre-build validation with real users replaces estimates with behavioral evidence before engineering investment begins.
